All right, boys and girls, I know all y'all been getting a lot of questions from clients over the tenure of your career. We always get tons of questions, always, whether we want them or not, right? So on one side of the coin, this can be irritating. On the other side of the same coin, though, it's the best opportunity we have to build, trust, and rapport with the client. If the client comes to the practitioner with a question, that's good. It means the client trusts us and cares enough to ask. Since Q&A is the bulk of the communication, at least in the beginning, it is important to know that extra care be taken in crafting your responses. In the beginning, this is the lifeblood of the relationship you have with your client. Make sure to get candid and flavorful answers appropriate to the client's education level. Sounding like a textbook will hurt you. Embrace analogies and metaphors and add personal flavor to the responses. Honestly, you can search for me in the groups, and I always try and act like as much of a doofus as I possibly can because that's what I like to do, and I also feel like it simplifies answers, but also communicates the point I want to get across. That being said, this isn't a dog and pony show. You always want to make sure you give a basis for any decision you make on the client's behalf. Behavior changes, accountability assignments, adjusting the plan. If you're trying to explain something to somebody, give them direction. Give them direction. And if the person has any questions, let them ask. But don't try and answer a bunch of questions for them before they come out because there's a million questions a person can ask. There's no way you can answer them all. So chances are, you'll just be wasting breath and you'll overwhelm you and the client. So just if the client wants to know why, they will ask, and if they don't, then they won't. And move right along. Simple as that. You want to operate under the premise of bi-directional trust. When trust is bi-directional, then interestingly enough, when trust is bi-directional, both sides trust each other, over-explanation is no longer required. But personality is. That is to say, if you ask a person a question and you trust that person, you shouldn't have to make them break out the textbook. If I know somebody who's an expert in physics and I ask them a physics question and they give me a very simple answer, I'm not going to have them break out the whiteboard and explain why. I'm not qualified to know the answer or the logic behind it. I just have to be able to trust that they know. And so once you have built that trust with somebody, then you can give short, direct answers and so long as they understand the sort of tone that you give them in, it will be all that is required. And if the person requires rationale, like I said, they will ask. The expert will cut right to the chase, but still provide a response in an entertaining and informative way. So if a person asks you a science question, give them a science answer, but don't read paragraphs from your medicinal biochemistry book for Pete's sake. Most times they are satisfied with concise, quippy responses. Like, oh, what do you think about intermittent fasting? Well, I sleep every night and break my fast with breakfast, right? Ha ha, right? So this is just how you communicate the importance of some topics and other ones might take more energy. So when you're responding to an inquiry, take two things into account. Okay? What is the client's preferred method of communication? What method of communication is best for the response? These are your two priorities. Phone calls, text, video chats, pre-made videos, books, articles, Facebook groups, other social media outlets, it doesn't matter. Use whatever medium is necessary to provide whatever information is required to qualify the decision you made. If the question can effectively be answered through the client's preferred medium, this is good. If a person prefers calls or prefers videos or prefers text, then use whatever method they prefer. If the question requires an alternative method, then give a brief explanation of the answer through their preferred communication method and then refer them to the alternative material if a more thorough explanation is required. So if a person was like, why do my muscles burn when I exercise, you can give a short response saying essentially the pH of your muscles changes like the acidity levels. You can probably use language like that, not necessarily pH, but acidity. And then if they say, well, why do my pH levels change when I exercise? That's not something that you can peck out on a cell phone, right? At that point, you might want to refer to an excerpt in a textbook and be like, okay, if you want to learn the science behind this, here's a science resource for you. Now this may seem intuitive, but it's not as straightforward as you think unless the process is laid out. So many off the cuff questions require massively complicated answers. Basically the more vague the question is, the more complex the answer has to be. Okay, soon you'll find yourself spending an hour crafting an essay for a person that has the question as benign as, are organic foods healthier? And man, that's either a one word answer or a one book answer. Am I right? I would know because I've written books worth of answers to these questions. So you may be years into your career already and haven't done it yet. That's okay. You're in a good company. It would take me a few years into my own career before I was in depth and kept records of my own answers. So how do you match the sort of method to the question? So here's a good set of guidelines to follow. If you get an email, respond with an email. You may need to include an attached audio or visual component. That's fine. If you have a prerecorded response, use it. If you can record a response to use it for another client and add to your Q&A database then totally do it. Refer to your Facebook group as often as possible. It is a great resource for a group coaching components. If you get a text message, respond via text. Again, you may include an audio visual component because most text messaging platforms now allow you to send voice and video messages. And again, if this is something where a group component would be helpful, then you can refer them to whatever social media group you have. Now in the very beginning, no matter what platform you use, for the first month or so, you're going to want to opt for video and voice responses as often as possible. This allows the client to adapt to your personality type and understand the way you communicate. It will allow you to adapt to your client's personality type. Your client will be able to read your emails and texts when your tone. So if you send videos and voice messages enough, then you can send a text message to somebody and they'll hear it or read it in your voice in their head. And that allows you to communicate very fast later on in your coaching relationship. And secondarily or alternatively, you'll be able to read emails and texts in the client's tone as well. And this is especially important with older clients, people who are essentially born before the internet. The way that they communicate via writing is way different than we do now with tons of emojis and other things that allow us to communicate our feelings. Slowly transition into text-based platforms the longer the client is with you. The client is going to have fewer questions, they will have more reasonable questions with the questions that they do ask, and they'll require less justification from you, like I said earlier, because they will trust you more. And lastly and most important, the client will be able to read your answers that you give them in your tone. So in a lot of cases, going back and forth with text is much faster than having a phone call. That being said, a 10 minute phone call can erase maybe hours worth of text messaging back and forth. And so if you front load those video and audio messages so the person gains trust in you and understands your tone, then you can transition to a more efficient communicating platform the later you go. If a client asks a question that will take more than 10 minutes to respond by typing it out, then send a video. If the question is more personal, then schedule a call because a lot of stuff you might not want to have in writing. If it is reasonable to record it for them, then okay, ask permission and you can send that recording back to them after you have the call. If something personal is currently happening or has happened to the client, always go for voice messages where they can hear you, schedule a call with them and send snail mail if something serious has happened, either happy or sad. Get married and get baskets, condolences, teddy bears, whatever the case may be, don't underestimate the power of getting something in the mail. It's very powerful. If a question requires a mechanistic response and recommend an article, so that's like, why do my muscles burn? If the client wants an explanation that's really scientific and hardcore, then send them to a scientific and hardcore reference to answer that question. If a client asks for a resource on an entire subject, like, hey, I want to learn nutrition, then okay, give them a reference for a nutrition book, that's okay. If basically, if the client is asking for a complicated explanation, then give them a complicated explanation. If they do not ask for a complicated explanation, then keep it to your damn self. Some clients will be placated by super advanced explanation even if they don't understand it, so you're going to have to be able to read a person there. Some clients want to seek further understanding of an explanation that's out of their current competency of the subject, that's okay. Humor them, send them resources. These clarifying questions also serve as a great outline for you to simplify your explanation for the next client.
